sutta: the buddha said monks these 4 persons are found existing in the world what for here in monks a certain person is a self tormentor given to the practice of self torment here and again a certain person is a tormentor of another given to the practice of tormenting another yet again a certain person is a tormentor both of self and another and given to that practice and yet again monks a certain person is a tormentor neither of self nor of another nor given to the practice of tormenting self and another he who torments neither united self he who torments neither self nor another in this very life is no more hungry he is allay he has become cool 1 who has penetrated bliss who dwells with self that has become brahma and how monks is a person a self tormentor 1 given to the practice of self torment in this case a certain 1 goes naked he has loose habits he licks his hands clean he will have none of your come in your reverence or stop a bit your reverence he refuses food brought to him he refuses special food he refuses an invitation to a meal he refuses food straight from the pot or straight from the pan or within the threshold of a door or among the firewood or among the rice pounders he refuses food when a couple are eating or from a pregnant woman or from a woman giving suck or from 1 sleeping with a man he refuses food from a mixed collection or where a dog stands by or when flies are swarming he eats neither fish nor flesh drinks no liquor not intoxicant not even rice gruel stop here for a moment here the buddha is describing those unbeneficial ascetic practices that are practiced by these external aesthetics who torment themselves like they inflict uh they they believe in doing all these practices which actually bring on suffering for example he goes naked licks his hands and then when people call him to come in he refuses to come in when people ask him to stop he refuses to stop he refuses special food and then he's got a peculiar sense of compassion when a couple are eating he refuses to take food from them maybe he thinks that they will not have enough to eat when a pregnant woman wants to give him food he refuses maybe he thinks that the baby inside that woman might not get enough nourishment when a dog is standing by he refuses to accept food because he thinks that the dog might have less food to eat because he accepts food when flies are swarming also he refuses because he thinks of compassionate thoughts towards the flies etc then the buddha continue he asks arms from 1 house only he's the eater of 1 mouthful or maybe he bags from 2 houses eats 2 mouthfuls 3 houses 4 up to 7 houses and eats 7 mouthfuls only he exists on just 1 little doll of food or just 2 or 3 or 7 such dolls he takes food only once a day or once in 2 days 3 days or once in 7 days thus he gives he lives given to the practice of taking food by rule even up to the extent of 2 weeks or half a month between meals he feeds on vegetables on millet on rice on raw rice on scraps of leather on water plants rice powder burnt scump of rice a flower of all seeds on grass and cow dung he just exists on forest roots and fallen fruit he wears coarse hampered clothes cloth of different fibers discarded corpse cloths rags from a rubbish heap tree bark fiber antelope skins strips of antelope skin explodes made of kusa grass made of wood shavings blankets made of human hair made of horse hair made of owl's wings he's a plucker out of hair and beard and given to this practice he remains standing and refuses a seat he squats down and keeps the squatting position he is a bed of thorns man he makes his bed on spikes he lifts given to the practice of going down to the water to bathe even to a third time in the evening also thus in diverse ways he lives given to these practices which torment the body thus monks is a person as self-tormentor and given to that practice they stop here just to comment again uh here if he begs for his food he picks maybe from 1 house or 2 houses only or 3 houses up to 7 houses maximum and this is not a buddhist practice but later books they got confused with these external ascetics and they said that buddhist monks are supposed to pick from 7 houses only this is definitely not buddhist practice is an external practice that crap into the later buddhist books and also he takes 1 food once a day or then or when he can do it then 2 days once and then when he can do that 3 days once up to 14 days he take 1 meal but when he takes that meal he'll gorge himself as much as he can and then after that he'll go on a fast and then he takes particular types of food even eating cow dung and fallen fruit etc and then he wears different kinds kinds of animal skins grass etc then he uses human hair to make a blanket and plucks out his his hair and his beard is quite painful but there's all these things and how monks is a person a tormentor of another and given to that practice hearing a certain person is a butcher a pig killer a fowler a deer stalker a hunter fisherman a bandit an executioner a jailer or 1 any of any other who follows a bloody calling that monks is how a person tortures another and is given to such practice of torture and how monks is a person a tormentor of both self and another and given to the practice of tormenting self and another here in monks a certain person is a raja or noble man who has been consecrated or a brahmana of a great house having built a new mood hall on the east side of a town he gets his head and beard shaved dawns a shaggy skin smears his body with ghee and oil scratches his back with a deer's horn and enters the moat hall together with his chief wife and brahmin chaplin there he makes his bed upon the bare grass covered ground then the raja lives on the milk from 1 teeth or nipple of a cow with calf of light color his chief wife lives on the milk from the second teeth the brahmin chaplin lives on the milk from the third teeth while dead from the fourth they offer a sacrifice to the fire the calf is fed with what is left over then he says let so many bulls be slain for the sacrifice that's so many cows so many heifers so many goats so many rams that so many horses be slain for the sacrifice let them cut down so many trees for sacrificial posts let them mow so much kusa grass for the sacrifice then whosoever are called his slaves messengers and workmen scared by fear of the rod with tearful faces set about their preparations that monks is how a person is a tormentor of both self and another given to the practice of tormenting both self and another and how monks is a person neither the 1 thing nor the other given neither to the practice of tormenting self nor to that of tormenting others he who torments neither self nor another in this very life is no more hungry he is become cool he's 1 who has penetrated bliss he lives with a self that has become brahma i stopped here for a moment this word become brahma is brahma bhutta now this word brahma bhutta is mentioned in 1 suta in the aganya suta of the diga nikaya where the buddha said this designates the tata gata so that is the real state of a buddha the real description of a buddha it's not that buddha has gone to nibana there is nothing left there is something which the buddha says is very profound unfathomable very deep and that the buddha calls the brahma body or dharma body brahma become what dharma become so that is the also the state of an arahant then the buddha continued in this case monks arises in the world and arahant a fully enlightened 1 perfect in knowledge and conduct a welfare a world knower unsurpassed driver of men to be driven teacher of devas and mankind a buddha an exalted 1 he makes known this world with his devas its maras its brahmas with its host of recluses and brahmins of devas and mankind himself having thoroughly understood and realized it he teaches dhamma that is lovely in the beginning lovely in the middle lovely at the end both in the spirit and the letter he makes play in the holy life entirely complete and fully purified then a house father or 1 of a household or 1 born in some family or other hears that dhamma and so hearing acquires faith in the tata gata i'll stop here for a moment you see here a person acquires faith by listening to the dhamma and this faith is sadha sadha sometimes translated as unshakable faith so the hearing of the dhamma is extremely important to have a firm foundation faith in the buddha dharma and sangha and then buddha continued possessed of this faith so acquired he thus reflects oppressive is this household life a dusty path the way of going forth is free like the open air it is no easy thing for 1 living the household life to practice the holy life in all its completeness in utter purity like a polish shell how if i were to get my beard shaved and donning the saffron robe were to go forth from home to the homeless then he some time later abandoning the whole mass of his wealth whether small or great abandoning his circle of relatives whether small or great gets his beard shaved dons the saffron ropes and goes forth from home to the homeless he having thus gone forth having entered upon the way of life in the training followed by monks abandoning the slaying of creatures abstains there from he lives as 1 who has laid down the rod who has laid down the knife who has scruples his kind and has compassion for every living thing abandoning the taking of what is not given he abstains there from he lives as 1 who takes only what is given who waits for what is given he lives with the self that has become pure not by stealth abandoning the unchased life he lifts chest gives a life aloof abstaining from the sexual act from dealings with women full abandoning falsehood he abstains there from he speaks the truth and joins truth to truth unswerving reliable no deceiver of the world abandoning slanderous speech he abstains there from when he hears something at 1 place he spreads it not abroad elsewhere to cause dissension among these folk when he hears something at another place he spreads it not abroad elsewhere to cause dissension among these folk thus he reconciles those who are at variance and confirms the friendly he delights in harmony finds pleasure hearing rejoices in harmony and utters words that make for harmony abandoning core speech he abstains there from whatever speech is blameless pleasing to the ear affectionate speech that goes to the heart disobeying delights manifold pleases many for such speech does he utter abandoning idol babble he abstains there from he is 1 who speaks in season speaks of facts speaks sense speaks according to dhamma speaks according to vinaya he speaks words worth treasuring up that are seasonable reasonable discriminating and concerned with profit stop here for a moment here the buddha is talking that once a person has gone for the first thing that he does is to follow the training and the basics of it is keeping a good seal moral conduct and here in this description that i just read just now is 1 of the few places where the 2 types of verbal karma are described the first 1 is musavada this 1 normally people know musavada means lying that means we abstain we have to abstain from lying then the second 1 is pisuna vacha sometimes translated as land slander slanderous speech now this 1 sometimes people can get a wrong interpretation or wrong idea of it because the way it's translated slander sometimes people think it's talking bad about somebody else that's not the meaning here is described very clearly unless you hear something you go somewhere else and repeat it and cause 2 different parties to be uh to to to quarrel uh with each other so you spread this harmony so that is the meaning of bisuna vacha sometimes translated as slander and the third 1 is parusa core speech score speeches this 1 is generally understood and of course a harsh speech that is painful to the ear this unpleasing and the last 1 is that is uh idol speech what is sort of gossip and unbeneficial talk then the buddha continued he is 1 who abstains from injury to seed life and plant life lives on 1 meal a day refrains from food at night abstains from food at unseasonable hours from flowers scents anguins adornments and finery from shows of notch dancing and singing from bits high and broad from accepting gifts of gold and civil that means money from taking gifts of uncooked grain of uncooked flesh from gifts of women and girls female and male slaves of goats and sheep fowls and swine elephants cattle horses and mares he abstains from gifts of fields cultivated our ways from buying and selling sending messengers outgoing as such from teaching with scales copper vessels or measures from taking bribes to pervert justice from cheating and crooked ways he abstains from cutting flogging binding highway robbery plundering and deeds of violence i'll stop here for a moment this last partner on sila injury to seed life and plant life is is more of an indian custom and the buddha did not say that we cannot uh injure seed life and plant life in as as the other external uh ascetics uh uh do the buddha was more uh lacks on this in the sense that the buddha said that for month um for example if a fruit is offered then if a lay person cuts it or pokes it then the monk can eat even to extend not damaging the seed that means it is made allowable for a monk so the buddha did not actually uh was not strict in this to the extent of some external aesthetics who considered plant life as a living being a 1 faculty living being and then 1 meal a day this 1 meal a day is uh the buddha asks his disciples to practice it so that they don't become too too too fat because it's not conducive to meditation so and then other things like gifts of women girls female and male slaves although the buddha did not allow mangas not to accept slaves and women etc but the buddha did allow during his time for the king to send workers to help among so that sometimes in the vinaya we find that there's a whole village of work manna employed by the king to work for the monks i started to build a monastery buildings etc you know and the monks would tell them what to do but um they were paid by the king however uh even though the mouse would tell them what to do yet the monks would go among them and beg for their arms food and then another thing i like to mention here is gives he abstains from gifts or fields cultivated always this 1 is only for arable fields like that means plantations monks cannot accept them directly but there is a type of land that a monken or the sangha can accept directly that is land for a monastery because the buddha made it very clear in the vinaya rules that the monastery land and monastery buildings and household items in a monastery belong to the sangha they are considered sangha property to be kept for the sangha monks of the 4 directions not south east west present and yet to come that means to be kept in perpetuity for the sangha of the present and the future and these uh sangha property cannot be sold cannot be given away cannot be distributed unless it is sold to buy something similar for the sangha for example you sell a piece of land to buy a bigger piece of land or something for a monastery so this uh abstention from gifts of fields cultivated always is does not refer to monastery land is only for like arable land fields and plantations etc even though the monks cannot accept it directly it can be accepted for example by a buddhist society or lay people and used to support the monks but the there's an incident in the midnight which shows that the buddha accepted monastery land directly when the first monastery was given to the buddha king bimbisara presented a piece of land to the buddha and the buddha accepted it directly in the old indian tradition the buddha kept his hands in a cup the palms of his hands and the king bimbisara poured water directly into the buddha's hands and the buddha accepted on behalf of the sangha he is content with a rope sufficient to protect the body with arms food enough for his belly's need wherever he may go he takes these with him just as for instance a bird upon the wing wherever he may fly flies with the load of his wings even so a monk is content with a rope and takes these with him possess of this aryan mass of morals he experiences himself the bliss of blamelessness seeing an object with the eye uh i stopped here for a moment the second thing that the monk coffee faces contentment and uh to continue the buddha said seeing an object with the eye he is not misled by its outer view nor by his lesser details since coveting and dejection evil and profitable states might flow in upon 1 who lives with the faculty of uncontrolled he applies himself to such control sets a guard over the faculty of eye and attains control thereof hearing a sound with the ear or with the nose smelling a scent or with the tongue tasting a favor or a shaver or with body contacting tangibles or with mind cognizing mental states he is not misled by the outer view nor by their lesser details but since coveting and dejection evil and profitable states by flowing upon 1 who lives and with his faculties uncontrolled he sets a guard over the faculties attains control thereof thus possessed of this aryan restraint of faculties he experiences in himself unadulterated bliss stop here for a moment so here the third thing is guarding the sense doors practice restraint over the sense doors and the buddha continue and going forth and returning he acts mindfully in looking in front and looking behind in bending or relaxing in wearing his robe and bearing outer rope and bowl in eating drinking chewing and tasting and easing himself in going standing sitting sleeping waking speaking and keeping silence he acts mindfully he possessed of this stop here for a moment so this fourth 1 is satisfactory mindfulness and awareness then the buddha continued he possessed of this aryan mass of morals and this aryan restraint of the faculties and this aryan mindfulness and composure and this aryan contentedness resorts to a secluded lodging place a forest the root of a tree a hill ravine grotto or cave a channel field a jungle path an open space a heap of straw after his meal when he has returned from his arms round he sits cross-legged keeping his body erect and fixing his attention in front of him then abandoning the hankering after the world he abides with heart free therefrom having regard for the welfare of everything that lives he purges his heart of the taint of irreverent abandoning sloth and topper he remains freed there from wide awake mindful composed and purges his heart of sloth and topper abandoning restlessness and flurry he abides tranquil at heart in the inner self he purges his heart of restlessness and flurry abandoning doubt and wavering he abides as 1 who has transcended them no longer questioning this or then in things profitable he purges his heart of doubt and wavering so uh let's now going uh stop here for a moment the seclusion is viveka resorts to a secluded place and then after that he abandons the 5 hindrances that's called nivarana pahana thus abandoning these 5 hindrances these stains of the heart which cause the weakening of wisdom allude from sense desires allu from from evil conditions etc he attains and abides in the first second third and fourth ghanas then with heart calmed and purified cleansed and flawless void obtains grown soft and pliable fixed and come to utter peace he bends down his mind to the knowledge of how to destroy the ah he comes to know as as it really is this is dukkha this is the arising of dukkha this is the ending of dukkha this is a practice going to the ending of dukkha he comes to know as it really is these are this is the arising of asavas this is the ending of asavas it's the practice going to the ending of asavas and him thus knowing thus seeing his heart is released from the asava of sense desire his heart is released from the asava becoming his heart is released from the ass of our ignorance by release comes the knowledge i am released he comes to know destroyed his rebirth lived is the holy life done is what was to be done there is no coming to the state of things thus monks a person is neither a tormentor of self nor of another nor given to the practice thereof he being neither of these in this very life hungers no more he is become cool he experiences india he abides in the experience of bliss with herself that has become brahma so amongst these are the 4 persons found existing in the world that's the end of the suta
commentary:
so here in this last part of the suta you find several factors explained the first 1 is sila the second 1 is contentment the third 1 is guarding the sense doors then mindfulness and awareness then uh seclusion then abandonment of the hindrances and attainment of the 4 genres these these factors are group together are called charana sometimes translated as conduct 1 of the names of the buddha is vijaya charana sampano replete or complete in knowledge and conduct so this conduct is the holy path which is the the practice of the holy path now in this suta uh these factors that i've just read to you just now uh 3 factors are not in here and these um in other words there are 7 factors here mentioned 7 factors and 3 are not mentioned and the 3 that are not mentioned is got from other sutas the first 1 is moderation in eating bhojani matanyuta the second 1 is devoted to wakefulness yoga the third 1 is the 7 [Music] true dharmas sadhama faith conscience remorse much learning energetic effort mindfulness wisdom now um the famous uh cydo ladies said or in his books he described this uh charana conduct he also took these 7 factors uh uh from the deega nikaya the the the kanikaya the factors given in the diga nikaya are just the 7 mentioned here and he missed out these other 3 and these other 3 are found in other sutas like majima nikaya and and also angutta nikaya like uh uh earlier i read that uh sure cause if a monk possess 3 things and he's on the sure course and these 2 things were these 2 factors were mentioned their moderation in eating and devoted to wakefulness and these uh 7 saddam is found in the majima nikaya so uh if you take these 7 so either the complete factors are for charana conduct or practice is actually the 7 which are found here namely sila contentment guarding the sense doors mindfulness and awareness seclusion abandonment of the hindrances and attaining of the 4 jaanas these are the 7 then you add another 3 moderation and eating devotion to wakefulness and the 7 true dharmas 7 sadhama um the other thing i'd like to mention here at the end of this suta is describe the ending of the asavas and the asavas mentioned here is the asava of sen's desire sensual desire kama saba then the asapa of becoming then the asava of ignorance so here there are only 3 things mentioned as asavas and then in some later sutas for example in the some other sutas they mentioned 4 and they mentioned 4 plus another 1 i think on views
